data.epi_content:
I'll see you again.

In the world I used to live in, people with common moral values would have considered my brother and father deserving of death.

At least here, they weren't quite bad enough to deserve death.

Slaves weren't considered people, and while someone might think it somewhat wrong to treat livestock harshly, they wouldn't consider it a capital offense.

Erica thought the same way.

"Erica, stop right there."

"Stop what?"

"No matter what, speaking that way about people who have already passed away..."

That's not really important anymore. I've thrown all the things I used to consider important into the gutter.

Most people prioritize themselves first. I'm just setting some things aside for my own sake, so why are you confronting me about it?

"...It doesn't matter, just listen. This is all karma. It's just starting to explode in my generation."

If I hadn't entered this lady's body and searched through her memories, I probably wouldn't have known this.

"The coffee we're drinking after our meal, the sugar in the pastries, the cotton used to make warm blankets. Where do you think it all comes from? Vivian, where do you think the sugar and tea leaves you use in your favorite pastries come from?"

When I singled out Vivian with my question, she hesitated before answering.

"...I suppose they come from farmers?"

"Ha, that's a funny joke. ...Hmm. Oh, farmers are farmers?"

But in this Empire, they would be quite different from farmers who till their own land.

"At least they don't come from the Schwerin family who spout nonsense about not fighting those who don't ride horses or carry weapons. They have plenty of farmers there too, but have you ever heard of them growing sugar or tea?"

Vivian shook her head.

When I mentioned Evan's family, he spoke to me as if he were tired. Perhaps he was telling me to stop.

"Why do you keep bringing up my family?"

I pondered Evan's question for a while. Then the exact reason came to mind.

"Why? Because I'm jealous. Mecklenburg had no such thing as honor. We sent expendable soldiers who didn't matter if they died by the dozens, turned all those Blacks who were barely using stones as weapons and eating people into slaves, and made this Empire prosperous. While you were playing your honorable knight games, we were doing all the dirty work to gain wealth."

We grew to a similar size as the neighboring great houses, but they were noble, and we were utterly barbaric. Perhaps Father had a slight deficiency in that area too. Obsessively seeking refinement, etiquette, and manners while never speaking of honor.

I think that's how the character of Erica Mecklenburg was created, all twisted up.
data.epi_content2:
"You all could just maintain your size and stay where you were. Your tax revenue alone could support an entire order of knights. But not our family. So we went to sea...!"

Though the family is already completely ruined, I'm not sure if it's me or this young lady speaking, but the words came out full of pride and joy.

"We went to sea and turned useless lands where only people lived into something more useful than anything else. We captured plenty of slaves, made them work on plantations without paying a single coin, whipped them, and cut off their wrists if they didn't listen. Just one hand, of course! If we cut off both, they couldn't work. Even if they died, they had to work until their dying breath. People called it cruel, but handling livestock is the owner's prerogative."

Vivian looks somewhat deranged after hearing what I said. Well, she probably thought sugar and nutmeg were grown just like wheat or barley, so hearing about such cruel atrocities must be quite shocking.

"That's how our family became great."

Why, there's probably someone of runaway slave origin around Vivian. And Vivian naturally thinks that slaves are people too. No man would explain to this woman who seems to have grown up in a flower garden how horrific the place he escaped from was.

"We were doing so well, then collapsed in an instant. Like someone stepping on and killing an ant that was wandering around."

Perhaps the Emperor looked at our insignificant family and couldn't stand seeing us acquire so much wealth.

"Up to this point, it could happen. You might think that misfortune upon misfortune means God is trying to completely destroy the evil Mecklenburg family."

"...Erica, no matter what, it's still your family. It's your roots. To speak of them as if they're worthless..."

"Why would a family collapse overnight due to religious fanatics, dragons, and rebellions? Pfft. Evan, if a family can be ruined by cripples with only their right hands as adult males starting a rebellion... then yes, it's a useless and worthless family."

It's just a place that couldn't possibly fall naturally but collapsed for the sake of a poetic justice story.

"Anyway, all sorts of idiotic things happened. What's the point of not denying my roots? It's all unnecessary. I just want to be able to live happily somehow. But this cursed family name follows my name and binds me."

"Still, you shouldn't talk like that. Especially you, who used to impose your standards on others."

Even if I say all this, the result will be the same. I didn't impose my standards on others; I wanted them to act according to common sense and decency.

I never forced anything, just gently suggested with words.

"I never forced anything!!"

When I shouted emotionally, everyone around looked at me. Most of those gazes were the kind that look down on someone beneath them, or filled with worry, sympathy, and some with contempt.

Among them, Vivian seemed quite shocked. Her usually bright eyes were slightly wet, looking at me with a face that seemed about to cry.

She kept trying to speak to me, flailing her hands in the air, then stopping, only to repeat the process. And I hate even that sight.

"You can easily say words that deny me, while saying that someone as worthless and wretched as me shouldn't even speak!"

"When did I ever say that?"

Ah, perhaps I didn't say such things after my head flew off in the marketplace.

"Right, when did I say that?"
data.epi_content3:
At those words, Evan made a dumbfounded expression, then looked at me as if I were completely insane.

"Why not? With your precious magic, you could cut off my head and knead my brain to find out what happened."

I trace my finger across my forehead, smiling in a way that might seem slightly refreshing.

"How about cutting this open? If you open the lid and check the contents, you can easily tell whether what I'm saying is true or false."

Saying that, I pulled my hair hard. As if suffering from hair loss, the hair came out weakly with a slight tearing sound. Looking closely, the hair wasn't pulled out; it just broke off weakly and remained in my hand.

I look at Evan and speak quietly. If one doesn't listen carefully, it might sound like I'm just muttering to myself.

"Evan, I hate you. Your actions are hopelessly ambiguous, yet you're wrapped up in your pathetic self-esteem, and I hate you even more because I can't forget the things you said to me."

Next, I look at Vivian and speak a little louder than before.

"Vivian, I hate you. You keep approaching me even though you know I like Evan, and that I dislike you because of it. I hate you more because you have no malice. Every time I envy and covet you, I want to die. And I'm so envious of how you don't care about others' love."

I don't know if I really hate them, but anyway, I've expressed what was in my heart. It's the end anyway.

In the distance, Lydia was walking toward the classroom with the children she was taking care of, having finished lunch. That insignificant child looked at Evan and surveyed the surroundings, and seeming to sense the bad atmosphere, tried to avoid the place.

As Evan approached Lydia to say something, I took out an object I had kept in my bag. As they exchanged words, Lydia seemed to flare up too, and they began raising their voices, shifting the attention that had been on me to them.

I stand up and naturally walk toward the two.

And I first fired one shot at Lydia. Like a drop of red paint on her stomach, the red color spread in a circle. Clutching her stomach, she leaned her back against the wall and slid down, and I put the gun directly against her forehead and pulled the trigger. It exploded.

Evan and Vivian look at me with shocked expressions. Next, I pointed the gun at Vivian. Evan chanted a magic spell, unable to attack me, instead casting a protective spell over Vivian.

Our eyes met alternately. I smile in a way that might seem slightly frivolous.

Actually, I've never blown my head off in one go, so I don't know if it will hurt or not. Ah, but shooting yourself in the head with a gun is a really messy way to die.

I don't like it. But I hate this situation even more.

Unable to pull the trigger, I change the direction of the gun. I felt a cold sensation against my temple.

If dying could turn back time, if I could escape unhappiness that way, then I should do it.

Even if I have to repeat it multiple times.

This time, it's not about escaping through death. I've already realized that I can't escape.

For others, it's just a result, but for me, it's the process to achieve a vague purpose.

"Let's meet again."
